medical_records.primary_topic: Acute Fever (7 days or less) without clear focus
clinical_signs: Seriously ill with no obvious apparent cause
Hypotension or shock
Maculopapular haemorrhagic petechial rash
Positive malaria test (RDT or microscopy)
Constipation or diarrhoea, abdominal pain, rose spots
Headache, stupor, eschar, tick exposure
Pain behind the eyes, backache, arthralgia, petechiae
return_to_clinic_warning_signs: Fever does not improve or comes back after treatment
Accompanied by cough, severe pain, confusion, stiff neck, or change in consciousness

medical_records.primary_topic: Prolonged Fever (More than 7 days) without clear focus
clinical_signs: Loss of weight, night sweats, malaise
Cough greater than 2 weeks
Limb pain, local limb tenderness and swelling
Low grade fever, new or changing heart murmur, signs of embolic disease
Right upper quadrant pain or tenderness
Contact with farm animals, unpasteurized milk
Enlarged lymph nodes, hepatosplenomegaly

medical_records.primary_topic: Bilateral Limb Swelling or Generalized Swelling
clinical_signs: Dyspnoea, orthopnoea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea
Pitting oedema of legs
Crackles in chest, elevated JVP
Hypertension, oliguria, macroscopic haematuria
Anasarca with periorbital oedema, heavy proteinuria
Jaundice, palmar erythema, spider angiomata, caput medusae, ascites
Wasted appearance, hair loss, hyperpigmentation, low serum albumin
Unilateral neck nodes, may be fluctuant or discharging, night sweats, weight loss
Painful, red, inflamed node with local source of infection
Tender inguinal lymphadenopathy, history of genital ulcers
Dark purple, painless lesions or nodules
Non-tender, enlarging nodes, systemic symptoms (fever, sweats, itch)
Petechiae, pallor, splenomegaly, extreme fatigue
suspected_conditions: Sexually transmitted infection (e.g. chancroid, LGV, syphilis)

step_by_step_guide: Confirm the diagnosis of opioid dependence using history, physical examination, and urine analysis.
Initiate Methadone at a low dose (generally 20 mg, or 10 mg if tolerance is low) to avoid overdose.
Alternatively, initiate Buprenorphine at 4 mg only when objective signs of opioid withdrawal appear (at least 6-24 hours after last opioid use).
For Methadone, observe the patient daily in the first week. Maximum daily increase is 5-10 mg, with max dose of 40 mg by the end of week one.
Titrate to an optimal maintenance dose (60-120 mg for methadone, 12-24 mg for buprenorphine).
Provide psychosocial support, vocational training, and counseling during maintenance.
If reducing or ceasing OST, decrease gradually (e.g., methadone no faster than 5 mg/month) to avoid withdrawal and relapse.

side_effects_or_warnings: Hypersensitivity reaction (2-5% of patients) presenting with fever, fatigue, rash, sore throat, or shortness of breath
Never reintroduce Abacavir after discontinuation due to hypersensitivity, as re-exposure may lead to severe and potentially fatal anaphylactic reaction

medical_records.adult_dosing: Standard dose is 400/100 mg twice daily. Take with a meal.
5 mg/kg body weight daily (range 4-6 mg/kg); Maximum 300 mg daily. For intermittent 3 times per week dosing: 10 mg/kg (max 900 mg).
side_effects_or_warnings: Peripheral neuropathy (burning, numbness or tingling sensation in hands or feet)

medical_records.adult_dosing: 10 mg/kg body weight daily (range 8-12 mg/kg); Maximum 600 mg daily.
side_effects_or_warnings: Orange/red discoloration of urine and secretions (harmless)
Strong inducer of cytochrome P450; decreases efficacy of oral contraceptives and contraindicated with unadjusted boosted protease inhibitors
Require dose adjustment in renal failure

clinical_signs: Onset over hours to days
Fluctuating level of consciousness within the day (often worsening at night)
Prominent difficulties in focusing, shifting, or maintaining attention
Difficulty orienting to place, time, and sometimes person
Agitation or withdrawal
Evidence of underlying medical cause, infection, or drug/alcohol withdrawal
Distal, symmetrical glove and stocking distribution of sensory changes
Painful tingling, burning, or numbness starting in toes or soles
Usually no motor weakness
Reduced or absent ankle jerk reflexes
Impaired vibration sensation and temperature perception

step_by_step_guide: Evaluate and treat or remove the underlying cause (e.g., substitute offending drugs like d4T or ddI with AZT or TDF).
Give thiamine if there is a known history of alcohol use.
Give pyridoxine 50 to 75 mg daily if the neuropathy is related to HIV infection or INH use.
Initiate Amitriptyline for neuropathic pain control: start at 25-75 mg at night, titrate up to 300 mg daily as tolerated.
If amitriptyline is poorly tolerated or ineffective, use Carbamazepine (start 100 mg twice daily, increase up to 1600 mg daily).
In HIV-positive patients on ART with poor response, consider Gabapentin starting at 300 mg daily, titrating up to 3600 mg daily.

clinical_signs: Flat or raised fleshy growths anywhere in the anogenital area (HPV)
Flat, moist, malodorous, warty-like lesions (Condyloma lata)
Dome shaped papules with central depression (Molluscum contagiosum)
Non-healing penile lesion, indurated and ulcerating (Penile cancer)
Painless indurated clean ulcer (Primary syphilis)
Painful ulcers with ragged undermined borders and fluctuant bubo (Chancroid)
Painless progressive beefy red ulcer bleeding easily on contact (Donovanosis)

step_by_step_guide: Use syndromic management protocols for genital ulcer disease.
Administer first-line therapy: ciprofloxacin 500 mg orally twice daily for 3 days; OR erythromycin 500 mg orally 4 times daily for 7 days; OR azithromycin 1 gram orally as a single dose.
If first-line fails or as an alternative, give ceftriaxone 250 mg IM single dose.
Review the patient in 3-7 days to assess healing. If healing is poor, treatment should be repeated or prolonged (especially in HIV-positive patients).
Ensure partner notification and treatment.
Stage the disease using CSF examination. First stage: CSF leukocytes up to 5/ul without parasites. Second stage: Trypanosomes in CSF or leukocytes >5/ul.
For First stage T. b. rhodesiense: Administer Suramin IV.
For Second stage T. b. rhodesiense: Administer Melarsoprol IV (2.2 mg/kg/day for 10 days). Monitor for reactive encephalopathy in an intensive care setting.
For First stage T. b. gambiense: Administer Pentamidine deep IM (4 mg/kg/day for 7 days).
For Second stage T. b. gambiense: Administer combined treatment of oral Nifurtimox and IV Eflornithine.

clinical_signs: Itchy maculopapular rash after freshwater exposure (swimmers itch)
Acute fever, chills, cough, muscle aches, and eosinophilia (Katayama fever)
Chronic intestinal disease: abdominal pain, bloody diarrhoea, tender hepatomegaly
Chronic urogenital disease: supra-pubic pain, dysuria, terminal haematuria
Late stage: Portal hypertension, ascites, oesophageal varices, cor pulmonale

step_by_step_guide: Confirm diagnosis via urine dipstick (microscopic haematuria), direct microscopy of stool/urine for eggs, or serology.
Administer Praziquantel 40 mg/kg as a single dose.
Administer the dose between meals to minimize adverse events like abdominal pain, nausea, and headache.
Repeat the dose after 4 weeks if follow-up microscopy shows evidence of ongoing infection.
If central nervous system involvement is suspected, refer the patient to a tertiary level facility for further investigation and treatment.

medical_records.primary_topic: Breast Care and Infant Feeding for HIV-Positive Mothers
home_care_instructions: Exclusively breastfeed your infant for the first 6 months if breastfeeding is the chosen infant feeding method.
Ensure you take your ARV prophylaxis or ART daily as prescribed to protect your infant from HIV transmission.
If replacement feeding is chosen, ensure you have safe water and sanitation at home, and exclusively give formula milk for the first 6 months.
Seek care promptly if you experience any breast problems, such as a breast abscess, mastitis, or nipple trauma, as these increase the risk of HIV transmission.
When you are ready to stop breastfeeding, transition gradually over about 1 month.
return_to_clinic_warning_signs: Redness, swelling, or extreme pain in the breasts
Infant develops diarrhoea or oral thrush
